missing_nonce_on_state_change
PHPDoc block describes wrong function signature — '@param $post_id (int)' and '@return $post_id (int)' are completely wrong for this method; this is a misleading public API docblock.

Unescaped output of developer-supplied HTML via printf() with $args['html_updated_message'] and $args['updated_message'] — even with phpcs:ignore, this pattern teaches unsafe output practices; the format string itself is unescaped and user/developer-controlled, creating XSS risk if args are not fully trusted.
Use of $_GET['updated'] without any sanitization or casting before use as a flag — even as a boolean check, this is a bad pattern to teach.

Missing form nonce output — while ACF may handle nonces inside acf_form_data(), this function as presented outputs a <form> element with no visible nonce field and no comment indicating where nonce is emitted, making it appear to teach nonce-less form rendering.
Use of json_encode() directly instead of wp_json_encode() which handles character encoding edge cases correctly per WordPress standards.
Loose comparison ($args['post_id'] == 'new_post') mixed with strict comparison ($post_id === 'new_post') for the same logical check — inconsistent and potentially buggy.
